active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.linprog with linear programming relaxation and iterative radius refinement
active_paradigms.description: Formulate the problem as a sequence of linear programs where each LP fixes the relative positions and solves for maximum uniform radius scaling. Use scipy.optimize.linprog to solve each LP, then iteratively adjust positions. This is fundamentally different from nonlinear optimizers because it uses linear programming which finds global optima for each subproblem. The approach alternates between: (1) fixing positions and finding maximum radii via LP with linearized distance constraints, (2) using the radii to guide position updates via force-directed repulsion. Start with hexagonal layout and run 50-100 iterations of LP solve followed by position adjustment.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: sum of radii through iterative LP refinement
active_paradigms.cautions: Linearize distance constraints at current positions, update linearization each iteration. Use small step sizes for position updates to maintain feasibility. Initialize with well-separated positions.

active_paradigms.idea: Use numpy.linalg.eig with spectral graph layout for initial placement, then local radius maximization
active_paradigms.description: Construct a graph where nodes are circles and edge weights represent desired distances (based on optimal hexagonal packing ratios). Use numpy.linalg.eig to compute the spectral embedding of this graph into 2D, which gives a globally optimal layout respecting distance constraints. Scale and shift the embedding to fit in unit square. Then compute maximum radii by solving the linear system of touching constraints. This exploits the mathematical structure of circle packing as a graph embedding problem. The spectral approach gives a deterministic, globally-informed starting point that respects the hexagonal packing structure.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: initial placement quality through spectral embedding
active_paradigms.cautions: Handle disconnected components in the graph. Ensure the eigenvector computation uses the correct eigenvalues (smallest non-zero for Laplacian). May need to rotate/flip the embedding to best fit the square.

active_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.ConvexHull with boundary-aware greedy placement and incremental radius expansion
active_paradigms.description: Construct the packing greedily by placing circles one at a time. For each new circle, use scipy.spatial.ConvexHull to identify the largest empty region boundary, then place the circle at the centroid of the largest gap. After all 26 circles are placed, perform radius expansion by computing the minimum distance to all constraints (boundaries and other circles) for each circle. This is a constructive geometric approach rather than iterative optimization. The convex hull helps identify boundary regions where larger circles can be placed. Use a priority queue to always place the next circle in the region with highest potential radius.
active_paradigms.what_to_optimize: greedy placement order and radius expansion
active_paradigms.cautions: Handle degenerate convex hull cases when fewer than 3 points. The greedy order significantly impacts final result - experiment with placing larger circles first vs. filling corners first. Allow slight position adjustments after placement.

tried_paradigms.description: Dual annealing is a simulated annealing variant that combines classical simulated annealing with a local search strategy. It's designed to find global minima in non-convex landscapes. Use it with bounds on all variables (centers in [0,1], radii in [0.001,0.5]), and pass SLSQP through minimizer_kwargs for local refinement. Set initial_temp=5230 and restart_temp_ratio=2e-5 for good exploration-exploitation balance.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Global optimum discovery through temperature-based search
tried_paradigms.cautions: Dual annealing minimizes, so negate the objective (maximize -sum_radii). Use visit=2.62 for proper annealing schedule. The no_local_search option should be False to enable local minimization. Set maxiter=2000 for sufficient exploration.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with trust-constr method and explicit constraint Jacobians
tried_paradigms.description: Trust-constr uses a trust-region sequential quadratic programming (SQP) algorithm with interior point method, which is fundamentally different from SLSQP's active-set approach. Define constraints using NonlinearConstraint with explicit Jacobian functions for the 78 overlap constraints and 104 boundary constraints. The trust-region approach handles nonlinear constraints more robustously near constraint boundaries. Use sparse Jacobian representation since each overlap constraint involves only 4 center variables and 2 radius variables. Start from the hexagonal pattern but also try perturbed versions with different random seeds.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: primary sum of radii, with better local minima from different constraint handling
tried_paradigms.cautions: Trust-constr requires proper constraint Jacobians for efficiency. Use sparse matrices for the Jacobian since constraints are local. Set ftol=1e-12 and maxiter=2000. Try both 'equality' constrained formulation (r_i = max feasible) and direct optimization.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.dual_annealing with adaptive penalty weights
tried_paradigms.description: Dual annealing combines generalized simulated annealing with local search, using a different exploration mechanism than basinhopping. Implement an adaptive penalty function: start with low penalty weights and increase them geometrically during optimization. The objective becomes -sum(radii) + penalty_weight * sum(constraint_violations). Use the 'lw' local search option with L-BFGS-B. Set initial_temp=5230, restart_temp_ratio=2e-5, and visit=2.62 for good exploration. Run multiple independent optimizations and keep the best valid solution.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: global search capability to escape local minima that trap gradient-based methods
tried_paradigms.cautions: Dual annealing doesn't support constraints directly, so penalty must be carefully tuned. Start penalty_weight small (0.01) and increase by 1.5x every 1000 iterations. Ensure final solution is validated and radii are recomputed using max_radii_for_centers.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.minimize with COBYLA method for derivative-free optimization
tried_paradigms.description: COBYLA (Constrained Optimization BY Linear Approximation) builds linear approximations of the objective and constraints, then solves a trust-region subproblem. This derivative-free approach can find solutions that gradient methods miss, especially near constraint boundaries where gradients may be misleading. Define all 182 constraints explicitly as inequality constraints. COBYLA handles constraints directly without penalty functions. Use rhobeg=0.1 and rhoend=1e-8 for fine convergence. Run from multiple starting points including the hexagonal pattern and random perturbations.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: different local minima from derivative-free search that may escape gradient-based traps
tried_paradigms.cautions: COBYLA can be slower than gradient methods, so limit maxiter to 5000. The method works best when starting from feasible points, so use the hexagonal initialization with small radii. After optimization, recompute maximum radii for the final centers.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.optimize.root with improved KKT formulation and warm-start from current best solution
tried_paradigms.description: Since scipy.optimize.root with KKT conditions already succeeded (+0.0046), enhance it by: (1) Use the current best solution as initial guess instead of random initialization. (2) Formulate the KKT system more carefully: stationarity conditions for the Lagrangian, primal feasibility for constraints, and complementary slackness. (3) Use the 'hybr' or 'lm' method with analytical Jacobian for faster convergence. (4) After solving KKT, verify and repair any slight constraint violations. The key insight is that KKT conditions directly target optimal solutions, avoiding the gradient approximation errors in the current smooth residual approach.
tried_paradigms.cautions: KKT system may have multiple solutions; use current best solution as warm-start; handle numerical precision issues in complementary slackness; verify solution validity after solving

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.Voronoi with alternating optimization between centers and radii
tried_paradigms.description: Implement a geometric approach using Voronoi diagrams: (1) Initialize circle centers with a good pattern (hexagonal or current best positions). (2) Compute Voronoi diagram with scipy.spatial.Voronoi to find nearest neighbors efficiently. (3) For each center, compute maximum radius as min(distance to boundary, distance to nearest Voronoi neighbor - that neighbor's radius). (4) Adjust centers by moving each toward the centroid of its Voronoi cell to potentially increase available space. (5) Alternate between computing max radii (via Voronoi) and adjusting centers until convergence. This exploits the geometric structure of the packing problem.
tried_paradigms.cautions: Voronoi computation for bounded regions requires clipping; handle edge cases where centers are on boundaries; iterate until radii stabilize; verify no overlaps after each iteration

tried_paradigms.idea: Use greedy incremental construction with largest-circle-first placement strategy
tried_paradigms.description: Implement a constructive approach that places circles one at a time: (1) Start with empty unit square. (2) For each of 26 circles, find the position that allows the largest possible radius by sampling candidate positions and computing max radius given existing circles. (3) Compute max radius at each candidate as min(distance to boundary, distance to each existing circle - that circle's radius). (4) Place the circle at the best position found. (5) After all circles placed, use scipy.optimize.root to locally refine the configuration. This greedy approach explores different solution structures than simultaneous optimization.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: constructive placement quality, local space utilization
tried_paradigms.cautions: Sampling density affects solution quality; early placements constrain later ones; use multiple random seeds for diversity; combine with local refinement after construction; handle edge cases near boundaries

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.Voronoi with Lloyd's algorithm for centroidal Voronoi optimization
tried_paradigms.description: Transform the problem into Voronoi cell optimization. For given centers, compute the Voronoi diagram using scipy.spatial.Voronoi. Each circle's maximum radius equals the inradius of its Voronoi cell (clipped to unit square). Use Lloyd's algorithm: iteratively move each center to its Voronoi cell centroid, which naturally maximizes cell regularity and inradii. The geometric structure ensures each circle uses maximum available space. This reduces the problem to 52 variables (centers only) with radii computed geometrically.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Maximize sum of Voronoi cell inradii through centroidal Voronoi tessellation
tried_paradigms.cautions: Handle boundary cells by clipping Voronoi cells to unit square. Use finite vertices at infinity. Iterate until convergence (centroid movement < 1e-8). May need random perturbations to escape local optima.

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.integrate.solve_ivp for physics-based circle expansion simulation
tried_paradigms.description: Model circles as expanding bubbles with collision dynamics. State vector contains [x1,y1,r1, x2,y2,r2, ...]. Define ODEs: dr_i/dt = 1 (uniform expansion), and position derivatives from collision forces. When circles overlap, apply repulsive forces proportional to overlap. When circles touch boundaries, apply inward forces. Use scipy.integrate.solve_ivp with Radau method (handles stiff dynamics from collisions). Integrate until kinetic energy dissipates. Final state gives natural packing where circles have found optimal positions through physics.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Maximize sum of radii through natural expansion dynamics until equilibrium
tried_paradigms.cautions: Use event detection to stop when system reaches equilibrium. Add small damping to position dynamics. Handle numerical stiffness from collision forces. Initialize with strategic positions (corners, edges, hexagonal interior).

tried_paradigms.idea: Use scipy.spatial.KDTree for greedy sequential circle placement with maximum radius computation
tried_paradigms.description: Construct the packing greedily by placing circles one at a time in positions that maximize their radius. Use scipy.spatial.KDTree for O(log n) nearest-neighbor queries. For each new circle: (1) Sample candidate positions on a grid, (2) Use KDTree to find distance to nearest existing circle, (3) Compute maximum radius as min(distance to nearest circle, distance to boundary), (4) Place circle at position with maximum radius. Prioritize large circles first (corners, then edges, then interior). This constructive approach builds optimal packings without iterative optimization.
tried_paradigms.what_to_optimize: Maximize each circle's radius at placement time using greedy selection
tried_paradigms.cautions: Grid resolution affects quality - use adaptive refinement near optimal positions. Order of placement matters - try different orderings (largest-first, corner-first). May need local refinement after construction. KDTree must be rebuilt after each placement.
